title: Six of Crows vs Crooked Kingdom: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Leigh Bardugo's Six of Crows vs Crooked Kingdom—which book in the duology is better? Complete comparison of books 1 and 2.

book1.title: Six of Crows
book1.author: Leigh Bardugo
book1.description: Ketterdam: a bustling hub of international trade where anything can be had for the right price—and no one knows that better than criminal prodigy Kaz Brekker. Kaz is offered a chance at a deadly heist that could make him rich beyond his wildest dreams. But he can't pull it off alone. A convict with a thirst for revenge. A sharpshooter who can't walk away from a wager. A runaway with a privileged past. A spy known as the Wraith. A Heartrender using her magic to survive the slums. A thief with a gift for unlikely escapes.

book2.title: Crooked Kingdom
book2.author: Leigh Bardugo
book2.description: Kaz Brekker and his crew have just pulled off a heist so daring even they didn't think they'd survive. But instead of divvying up a fat reward, they're right back to fighting for their lives. Double-crossed and badly weakened, the crew is low on resources, allies, and hope. As powerful forces from around the world descend on Ketterdam to root out the secrets of the dangerous drug known as jurda parem, old rivals and new enemies emerge to challenge Kaz's cunning and test the team's fragile loyalties.

table.book1: Impossible heist—breaking into the Ice Court

book1Strengths: The Ice Court heist is pure genius—breaking into an impenetrable fortress feels impossible until Kaz's plan unfolds
Kaz Brekker became YA's most beloved anti-hero (Dirtyhands, Bastard of the Barrel, 17 years old running the Dregs)
Each crew member brings distinct skills: Inej (the Wraith), Jesper (sharpshooter), Nina (Heartrender), Matthias, Wylan
Bardugo's worldbuilding shines—Ketterdam's Barrel district, Fjerdan Ice Court, Grisha powers feel lived-in and real
The heist structure delivers constant tension—every chapter advances the impossible mission with perfect pacing
book1Weaknesses: Flashback chapters interrupt momentum—Kaz's trauma with Jordie, Nina/Matthias backstory slow the heist at times
You'll finish desperate for the sequel—that cliffhanger ending is brutal (Inej captured, crew scattered, mission failed)
Romantic threads barely start—Kaz/Inej have three conversations, Wylan/Jesper just hints, Nina/Matthias complicated
Some find 465 pages slow to start—first 100 pages assemble crew before the real action begins
book2Strengths: Kaz orchestrates 4+ simultaneous cons against Van Eck—the auction house scheme alone is breathtaking strategic brilliance
Every emotional payoff lands: Kaz touches Inej's hand (chapters of buildup), Wylan confronts his father, Nina grieves
The crew fighting on home turf (Ketterdam) showcases each member's expertise—no more foreign territory disadvantage
Bardugo sticks the landing—satisfying conclusion without cheap fixes, bittersweet but earned (Nina's sacrifice hurts)
Rated 4.6 vs 4.5—readers who finish both overwhelmingly prefer the sequel's emotional depth and complex plotting
book2Weaknesses: 200,000 fewer readers (385K vs 585K)—classic sequel drop-off despite being the better book by ratings
Tracking 4+ schemes simultaneously demands focus—Van Eck manipulation, Council politics, Black Tips gang war, auction
536 pages with slower middle section—some political maneuvering drags compared to book 1's relentless heist momentum
Cannot work as standalone—you'll be completely lost without Six of Crows backstory and relationships established

book1: The heart is an arrow. It demands aim to land true.
I'm a business man. I don't trade in blood.
Greed bows to me. It is my servant and my lever.
When everyone knows you're a monster, you needn't waste time doing every monstrous thing.
I would have come for you. And if I couldn't walk, I'd crawl to you, and no matter how broken we were, we'd fight our way out together.
book2: I'm going to get my money, Kaz vowed. And I'm going to get my girl.
You were angry. You are angry. That's what you do best.
Better terrible truths than kind lies.

Some people see a magic trick and say, 'Impossible!' They clap their hands, turn over their money, and forget about it ten minutes later. Other people ask how it worked. They go home, get into bed, toss and turn, wondering how it was done.
I have been made to protect you. Only in death will I be kept from this oath.
Stay. Stay in Ketterdam. Stay with me.

book1: Experience one of YA's best heist stories
The impossible heist structure is brilliant
Each character is complex and compelling
book2: Get the emotional payoffs and conclusion
Kaz's schemes are even more brilliant
Many fans consider it better than book 1
The highest-rated book in the duology

verdict.winner: Read Both (but Crooked Kingdom edges it)
verdict.summary: Here's something rare in publishing: the sequel outperformed the original. Crooked Kingdom earned 4.6 stars compared to Six of Crows' 4.5—readers who stuck with the duology overwhelmingly preferred the second book. Why? The emotional payoffs hit harder, Kaz's schemes grow more intricate, and Bardugo delivers a satisfying conclusion that doesn't feel rushed or convenient. But here's the catch: you absolutely cannot skip Six of Crows. With 585,000 reviews establishing the Dregs crew, the Ice Court heist sets up everything that makes Crooked Kingdom work. Think of them as one 1,001-page story split into volumes.
verdict.recommendation: Start with Six of Crows ($11.99, 465 pages, 4.5 stars, 585K reviews). The Ice Court heist introduces Kaz Brekker and his crew with perfect pacing—you'll understand why he became YA's most beloved anti-hero. Yes, the flashback chapters slow momentum occasionally, and that cliffhanger ending is brutal. But Bardugo's worldbuilding (Ketterdam's Barrel, Grisha powers, Fjerdan culture) creates the foundation you need. Then immediately grab Crooked Kingdom ($12.99, 536 pages, 4.6 stars, 385K reviews). Kaz orchestrating simultaneous cons against Van Eck showcases strategic genius that surpasses book one. The emotional moments—Kaz finally touching Inej's hand, Wylan confronting his father, Nina's sacrifice—land because you've invested 465 pages in these characters. Together: $24.98 for one of YA fantasy's best duologies. Read in order, no exceptions.
